[[File:277theo1.png|590px|link=]]












































{{User:A4f7/8new|We will pursue the terrorists everywhere. You will forgive me, but if we catch them on the toilet, we will wipe them out in the outhouse; In this way, all issues will eventually be solved.|Vladimir Putin, 1999, in Dagestan}}
The dry, splintering crunch of dead leaves beneath my feet pierced through the damp air, echoing at my ears like an unbearably sharp shriek. Perhaps it was because I had spent half my life drifting through countless forests, long since accustomed to the lethal dangers lurking beneath mottled shadows, and had witnessed time and time again the twisted ways they claimed their victims’ lives.

Even so, my heart still tightened with unease. No matter how many horrifying places I had escaped from, I could never be certain whether, in the next second, I would still remain alive as I expected. My anxious breathing carried through the surrounding woods, so I tried to steady it. It should have been easy, but for a multitude of reasons my thoughts remained hopelessly tangled—made worse by the deadweight tagging along beside me. “Hey, Miss Leticia.”That person walking beside me was named Stacy. Here’s a joke for you: she was a Hawaiian with Japanese ancestry, looking like some strange blend of Jake Shimabukuro and Hirano from Phineas and Ferb. A truly unfortunate coincidence, though I doubted many other wanderers would care. I knew perfectly well what she wanted to say; I’d known since we were still back in Level 483. Absurdly enough, I was the one who had found most of the almond water. “So, what do you want to ask?” I figured I’d throw her a little emotional validation. I’d already said enough anyway, and sure enough, she started accusing me of drinking too much of the stuff. If I had a choice, I wouldn’t even want to share a drink with her—I’d probably end up with E. coli. The cicadas crying through the forest sounded as though they were desperately mocking the absurd farce unfolding between us. Ironically, that piercing frenzy seemed less like celebration and more like a final, agonized farewell to the world—a dying scream wrung from them with the last scraps of strength before death. Somehow, it gave me a little courage. Maybe it gave her some too. I couldn’t be bothered to find out. She clicked her tongue and walked off, forcing me to follow. When she pushed aside the branches ahead, she paid no attention to whoever came behind; the rebounding twigs nearly lashed across my eye. It was obviously deliberate. Still, I had no intention of confronting her yet. She still needed me for certain things, because she hadn’t prepared nearly enough supplies to survive on her own—let alone wander these places safely. Then again, self-awareness isn’t something everyone possesses. “How much longer are we supposed to keep walking before we stop?” I wiped sweat from my face, unable to hide the irritation in my voice. She kept moving ahead without the slightest hesitation, not even sparing me a glance over her shoulder. Only her low, cold voice drifted back through the trees, carrying an unquestionable sharpness: “Shut up. Read the map yourself.”This area had been relatively well-trodden before, thoroughly explored, so naturally there were traces left behind. A few hours earlier, I’d handed her that ragged parchment map and told her to guide us. Now, I deeply regretted that decision. If I had to choose again, I’d rather gamble away my own prefrontal stability than deal with her—if only because I feared the torrent of insults she’d unleash. The cold light of the overcast sky filtered through layers upon layers of leaves, turning into long, blade-like streaks of light slanting across the ground and our bodies. Those shifting shadows resembled invisible whips, burning and oppressive, mercilessly lashing against our backs already soaked sticky with sweat, leaving behind stinging pain. I had never met someone so incapable of choosing a proper path. When I complained about how slippery the trail had become, the mud thick with moisture and difficult to cross, she merely let out a cold sneer filled with contempt and ridicule. Without restraint, she mocked me, saying that choosing to wear these shoes in the first place was my own fault, and that suffering now was simply what I deserved. Her argument was revolting. In a place starved of resources, how could anyone stand there so self-righteously and expect a struggling survivor to possess equipment capable of handling extreme conditions? From every angle, the logic was absurd. In an environment where even basic survival was a luxury, assuming others should own superior gear—and using their lack of it as ammunition for mockery—was a narrow-mindedness so severe it became disgusting. I did everything I could to widen the distance between us, until it became enough to sever any possible intersection. I deliberately lagged farther behind, until a gulf formed between us so vast not even our toes could brush against each other. Within that distance, I hoped to temporarily shut out the kind of malice that burned through neurons, to give myself a brief rest from the chaos. And yet, because of what she kept doing, I was forced to keep paying attention to her condition to some extent. It wasn’t something I wanted, but nobody can completely ignore a disaster unfolding before their eyes—especially one they can’t fully control. Watching a bomb that might explode at any moment was utterly exhausting. I wished I could instantly fly away to somewhere peaceful, someplace where I could just drink hot cocoa in silence. The air around us remained saturated with methane and the stench of decay. Amid that endless, sickly-sweet reek, I could almost see forgotten blueprints rotting with time, transforming into ruined husks just like the corrupted gas itself, collapsing beneath the shadow called regret. I wondered when the winds of change would finally begin to blow. I only hoped it wasn’t nothing more than a fantasy.

{{Underline|FFFFFF}}


































































[[File:fllorrfffdddfr.png|590px|link=]]





{{User:A4f7/8new|You think I don't know? When it's hero time, if I mess up, somebody could die. From what you told me, if we mess up this time, everybody could die... Maybe that's too much to have in your head when you have to win. Maybe if I pretend everything is a big joke, when the time comes, I'll be able to do what I have to do.|Ben 10: Ultimate Alien, S2E6}} A faint, muffled whimper drifted from somewhere beyond my sight. It sounded like a child. Personally, I had no desire to get involved. Protecting yourself matters more than anything else—even if the universe itself were to collapse, that would still remain one of the unchanging laws of existence. Besides, it could just as easily have been an auditory hallucination. The girl ahead of me finally stopped walking. At last, she understood that making others suffer could eventually circle back onto herself. Then she spoke: “There’s some weird sound coming from up ahead. Let’s go check it out.” Hearing that, I couldn’t help but sneer inwardly. So that was it. From beginning to end, the only thing she cared about was that strange little disturbance ahead. And here I was, foolishly thinking otherwise. So the two of us moved behind the trees. I broke into a run, but when I tried to stop, I planted my foot too hard. The sole instantly lost traction, and my body slid uncontrollably forward. For a split second, I was only centimeters from smashing into the ground, but thankfully my legs held at the last possible moment, steadying me before I fell. Then I saw her. A little girl in a pink dress, crying quietly to herself. I guessed she had gotten lost, and suddenly certain things from earlier started making sense. Still, I found it hard to believe. In a place overflowing with danger and uncertainty, how could anyone be dressed as though they were attending some afternoon tea party? And, from the looks of it, she seemed to have Asian ancestry too. By my usual standards, I wanted absolutely nothing to do with this problem. Unfortunately, the woman traveling with me clearly had other intentions. “Oh? You’re thinking of leaving?” she asked, immediately noticing my attempt to back away. Her voice carried open provocation. “I don’t want to take pointless risks,” I replied coldly, my gaze fixed firmly on the little girl. “What risk could there possibly be? She doesn’t even look seven years old.” “So you’re just discarding the possibility that she could be a risky?” I tried to reason with her logically, but she simply turned away, completely ignoring my warning. She crouched beside the child, softening her voice. “Hey, little one. It’s okay.” Then she asked gently, “Why are you here all alone? Did you fall... or did you just wake up here?” The little girl looked up at her with swollen, bloodshot eyes rubbed raw from crying. Patiently, she repeated the question. The girl only shook her head over and over, tears carving streaks through the dirt on her face as she sobbed: “I was walking... with my sister... and then... and then...” I knew this was the perfect chance to leave. We needed to keep moving. In a place like this, time was worth more than gold. Yet there she was, kneeling beside the child like some deranged version of the Virgin Mary overflowing with performative compassion. The hypocrisy was nauseating. I wanted to say we needed to keep moving, but instead I listened to her murmur gently: “Calm down, okay?” “She disappeared...” “Don’t be scared. I’ll help you find her, alright?” she told the little girl, without even glancing at me. At last, I snapped. I strode forward and yanked her aside by force. Out of the child’s sight, I hissed through clenched teeth: “What the hell are you thinking? We’re already almost out of supplies. Keeping just the two of us alive for three more days is barely possible, and you already criticized me over that. And now you want to bring along another person? You’re dragging her to her death!” She violently shook off my hand and immediately fired back, her sharp, venomous words pouring out like machine-gun fire. She accused me of being cold-blooded, selfish, even inhuman. The way she looked at me was like staring at a rotting corpse, devoid of even the slightest warmth. In her world, I had become the sole villain. The little girl sensed the tension between us. Carefully, timidly, she shuffled closer and reached out her filthy little hand, clutching tightly onto the hem of the woman’s clothes as though grasping the last floating scrap left to a drowning person. In her eyes swirled fear, dependence... and something hollow I couldn’t quite describe. Watching that scene, a powerful and indescribable unease crawled up from the base of my spine. More than anything, it was fear of where this would lead. That excessive compassion made me feel as though I were watching fate itself fracture before my eyes into blackened shards. I could already foresee it dragging every one of us into a bottomless abyss beyond salvation. That realization was truly terrible. “Leticia, hold this for me a second. I want to check on this poor kid.” “You only have one backpack, and you still want me to carry it?” But I took it anyway. I had no desire to revisit those arguments ever again. I accepted the heavy pack, the nylon straps biting into my shoulders, and watched her turn away, focusing all her attention on that tiny pink figure while I was reduced to the quietest—and most unwilling—porter in this place. The wind threaded through the treetops. At that moment, I genuinely wished I could use Greek Fire to burn this entire forest to the ground. I stood a few steps away, watching coldly as she reached out and gently patted the girl’s trembling shoulders. “It’s okay. It’s okay...” She repeated those utterly meaningless words of comfort as though, if spoken enough times, this malicious world—though, honestly, she herself seemed to be one of its most active participants—might somehow become safe. “By the way, what’s your name? And what’s your sister’s name?” “My name is Luxembourg... and my sister is... Freyrita.” Oh, Luxembourg. Rosa Luxemburg. What kind of parents chose a name like that? “That’s a very pretty name.” She began fumbling through her pockets, trying to find something that might comfort the child. To me, those hurried little gestures looked absurdly comical. I could feel time slipping away; every second we stayed here was like sinking deeper into a dangerous swamp. My heartbeat quickened, but not from sympathy—from anger and vigilance. I scanned the surrounding shadows, trying to distinguish any hidden threat lurking within them, gripping the backpack so tightly my knuckles turned white. “We can’t stay here,” I reminded her. “If we’re taking her with us, then hurry up. Otherwise, let go of this now.” She ignored me completely. Instead, she pulled the child even closer into her arms. In an old-world film, that scene might have looked like some deeply moving moment of salvation. Here, it was nothing more than a textbook example of suicidal stupidity. She continued asking the girl for details: where had her sister disappeared? Had she seen any strange markings? The child answered in broken fragments, scattered words drifting uselessly through the air without forming anything remotely useful. I forced myself to remain patient, feeling the soft, unstable earth beneath my feet and the mildew stench that never truly faded. Eventually, we started moving again—or rather, shuffling forward. Because of the child’s short legs and stumbling steps, our pace dropped to an infuriating crawl. She walked ahead holding the girl’s hand, while I followed behind carrying both backpacks like some exiled slave. We pushed through clusters of withered shrubs and stepped over pale fallen trunks that resembled enormous exposed spines lying across the trail. Every agonizing minute drove my sanity closer to collapse. I stared at their backs—at that tiny pink silhouette. The bright color looked painfully out of place amid the endless dead gray-brown surroundings. So vivid. So... unnatural. My gaze drifted downward involuntarily. Because we were moving so slowly, I had far too much time to study the scenery around me: a landscape utterly devoid of life. Then, as they stopped once more to debate which fork in the path to take, I lowered my head slightly to adjust the crushing weight on my shoulders. And in that moment, I saw—

{{Underline|FFFFFF}}


































































[[File:fllorrffffr.png|590px|link=]]





{{User:A4f7/8new|I SOLEMNLY PLEDGE to dedicate my life to the service of humanity|Declaration of Geneva, I}} I called out to Stacy, telling her to come over. There was something I needed to say. “What is it?” So I led her to the place where I had spotted the corpse. As for who this person was, I could already make a fairly accurate guess. “Holy shit... don’t tell me this is her siste—” “Who else do you think would be out here besides us?” I said, staring at the recently deceased body. To my eyes, the corpse looked like someone who had died from radiation exposure. But there were none of the immediate signs of acute high-dose exposure—no instant carbonization of tissue, no widespread catastrophic ulceration. It didn’t make sense for it to be acute poisoning. That left only one conclusion: chronic radiation damage from prolonged low-dose exposure. I glanced at the digital watch on my wrist. The date read March 12. It had been nearly two years since I graduated from the Department of Radiological Sciences. “We need to think about what to do next.” “Bury her.” “What?” “I’m going to get the girl first.” And just like that, she walked away again, leaving me alone with my thoughts. Surely I had misheard her. “You go stand under that big tree over there and count to one hundred, okay? No peeking.” That was her voice. I couldn’t understand it. In a situation already as terrible as this, why make the little girl play such a meaningless game? I watched the child obediently run toward the distant tree, the pink silhouette disappearing behind the trunk. The instant she was gone, the smile on Stacy’s face collapsed. She rushed at me, grabbing my shoulders with hands so cold and trembling from panic that her nails nearly dug into my flesh. “She can’t see it... If she finds out that’s her sister, she’ll break. She’ll lose her mind!” she whispered frantically, eyes stretched wide with indescribable emotion. Her voice carried a suffocating intensity. “We have to get rid of the body now—bury it, hide it, anything! We absolutely cannot let this story fall apart, do you hear me? We have to deal with it!” Oh. So I hadn’t misheard her after all. I stared at her in disbelief. In that moment, the woman standing before me—the one who kept claiming this was “for the girl’s sake”—felt a thousand times more horrifying than the corpse itself. This wasn’t saving someone. It was desecrating the dead while deceiving the living. She completely ignored the horror and opposition in my eyes and practically forced me to help her. We had no tools. Out in this wasteland, there wasn’t even a rusted shovel to be found. All we could use were sharp branches snapped from nearby trees and our own filthy hands, already packed with mud and blood beneath the fingernails, clawing into the rain-soaked, rotting earth. I felt sick. “This is covering something up! This is hiding a corpse! You’re not helping her—you’re just helping yourself avoid trouble!” “This is protection! This is the kindest lie in the world! A cold-blooded animal like you would never understand!” she shouted back while desperately wiping mud and tears from her face, stomping the overturned soil flat with all her strength. The body was buried hastily in a shallow pit, concealed beneath scattered dead branches and leaves until it resembled any other mound in the forest. She rose to her feet, scrubbed the mud from her face, took a deep breath—and somehow forced out a mournful smile, gentle like a grieving grandmother. When we returned to the little girl, she crouched beside her and spoke with soft regret: “I’m sorry, sweetheart. We couldn’t find your sister... but I think we’ll see her soon.” The last faint spark in the girl’s eyes died in that instant. What replaced it was a sorrow and emptiness so deep it felt capable of freezing the soul itself. “Come with me. Stay with me, okay? I’ll protect you in your sister’s place.” Without waiting for an answer, she took the child’s hand. A strange sense of satisfaction spread across her face. It wasn’t simple compassion. It looked more like someone who had finally found something to fill a crack within themselves—something far removed from actually saving another person. I walked silently at the back of the group. As we passed the freshly disturbed earth, my stomach twisted violently, and I nearly vomited up everything I had eaten that morning. That mound was a grave of sin, and we were all accomplices. The little girl moved like a puppet, allowing herself to be led along. She never spoke. Yet tears continued to stream endlessly from her eyes like broken strands of pearls, each heavy droplet striking the blackened, withered leaves beneath her feet. I stared at her fragile back. It wasn’t the back of someone who should ever have had to endure this. Finally, I couldn’t hold myself back anymore. I strode forward several steps and leaned close to the woman’s ear, practically snarling in a hushed voice: “You lunatic. You stole her last chance to say goodbye to her family—to see the truth one final time!” “What do you know?” she snapped, whipping around. Her restrained voice trembled with twisted pride. “Do you even understand psychological trauma? Watching someone you love die right in front of you becomes a shadow that follows you forever! I’m saving her childhood!” “This isn’t saving anything,” I shot back, pointing behind us. “There’s still time to go back. Dig the body up. At least then things can still be set right!” “You’re insane! You’re completely insane!” she screamed, her face contorting with fury. “If we go back now, then I become a liar! Do you want to destroy the way she sees me?” Our voices grew louder and louder, the argument devolving into outright venom. “You’re only doing this to satisfy your pathetic hero complex! You don’t care about that girl at all—you only care about preserving your image as some saintly benefactor in that shattered mirror of yours!” I tore straight through the paper-thin mask she hid behind. Her whole body shook like dead leaves in autumn wind. She raised a mud-caked hand and swung wildly at my face, but I reacted fast enough to catch her wrist in midair. She had already descended into near-mindless rage, and in blocking her attack, I completely forgot about the little girl in the pink dress. She was standing only a short distance away now, half-hidden within the cold shadows of the trees. Those once-hollow eyes were staring directly at us.

{{Underline|FFFFFF}}


































































[[File:277the7457.png|590px|link=]]





{{User:A4f7/8new|Until you've lost your reputation, you never realize what a burden it was or what freedom really is.| Margaret Mitchell, Gone with the Wind}} The air froze in that instant. The silence became so absolute it felt as though even a pin striking the ground would echo for miles. I watched the fear and sorrow in the little girl’s eyes ferment, collapse, and transform within a single second into something far more horrifying: pure, freezing hatred. “It was you... You killed my sister...” Her voice was quiet, yet colder than an ancient glacier, piercing straight into our bones. Stacy completely lost her composure. All the confidence she had carried shattered instantly. Stumbling forward, she reached for the girl’s shoulders in panic. “No—no, that’s not what happened, sweetheart, you misunderstood! We were trying to help you—we only wanted to protect you from getting hurt—” The little girl let out a shrill scream and violently slapped away the hands reaching toward her, staggering backward with the terrified expression of someone staring at two entities. “Don’t touch me! You murderers! Liars!” Her scream ripped through the stagnant forest air, trembling with despair. She tore herself free from Stacy’s grasp, and the look in her eyes as she turned away was one of utter betrayal and terror—like a deer cornered beyond escape. Without a second of hesitation, she bolted deeper into the woods. “Come back! It’s dangerous over there!” Our throats turned raw as we shouted after the vanishing pink silhouette again and again: “It’s not what you think!”
“Please, let us explain!” But in that tangled, chaotic terrain, terror itself seemed to lend the girl inhuman speed. Her figure flickered between the shifting shadows of the trees. Within only a few strides, the forest floor turned brutally steep. Thick weeds and slick moss concealed deadly fractures in the terrain beneath. That tiny pink form darted through the dim woods straight toward the edge of a moss-covered drop. My eyes nearly split open as I screamed with every ounce of strength I had left: “Watch out! Stop!” But the warning, soaked with regret, came half a second too late. What followed was a short, piercing scream sharp enough to tear across the sky itself, then the horrific sound of a body tumbling violently against hard stone—impact after impact after impact— And finally... The final sound of a life colliding with the dead earth below. She had fallen into a hidden chasm split open in the ground: a massive wound deep within the forest, bottomless and invisible beneath the vegetation. The two of us staggered toward the edge like empty shells stripped of their souls, our legs weak as we peered into the darkness below. There was nothing. Nothing except endless blackness and suffocating silence. Only the echoes of our self-righteous arguments remained, reverberating through the depths of the pit like mocking laughter.

{{Underline|E60000}}


































































[[File:277thyt47.png|590px|link=]]





{{User:A4f7/8new|Houston, we have a problem!|—Apollo 13(flim)}} “What... are we supposed to do now?”She spoke softly, tapping against the log she sat upon. Though there was uncertainty in her voice, her calm, sharp eyes still carried an almost detached rationality. Deep down, she clearly did not blame herself entirely for what had happened. Three full hours had passed since the accident. Everything was long over, yet she still sat there in a deathly daze. Personally, I thought she was simply trying to numb herself. A single raindrop fell from the heavy clouds above. It was overdue. That droplet had circled within those gray skies for far too long, swollen with unbearable weight. If it hadn’t fallen eventually, that would have been stranger. I looked up at the sky—not blue, never blue here anymore—and waited for the inevitable downpour. But... As far as I knew, there had never been any record of rain in this place. Then everything happened very quickly. The ground became slick almost instantly, and the rain continued without pause. The liquid struck my skin, and discomfort rapidly turned into pain. Something was wrong. I was absolutely certain of it. The droplets clung to my skin like diluted corrosive fluid. I raised my hand and examined it closely. Under the dim gray light, the liquid carried an unnatural murky tint touched with the faintest trace of green, as though mixed with fine sediment. It left pale marks across my skin that immediately reminded me of certain photographs I had once studied in old reports. The rainfall intensified rapidly, yet the sound remained strangely muffled. There was none of the normal crisp rhythm of rain striking leaves and earth. Instead, it resembled countless tiny heavy objects continuously crashing down upon the world, creating a low droning hum that accumulated within the air itself until it became a resonance sharp enough to make my eardrums ache. The ground beneath my feet became impossible to recognize. The once-soft soil transformed into slick mud, every step sinking into something half-solidified. Even the plants themselves seemed to be changing—the branches twisting, the leaves sagging unnaturally, the entire forest reshaped by some invisible force. For a moment, I couldn’t tell whether my senses were failing or whether the place itself was undergoing some irreversible transformation. The rainwater pooled far too quickly. Low ground flooded within minutes, but the liquid gathering there was anything but clear. It churned with murky turbulence, its surface rippling constantly as tiny bubbles rose and burst, releasing even fouler odors into the air. I tried to identify a safe route forward, but everything had already dissolved into grayness. No direction. No markers. Even the trail we had followed here had been erased completely by the unnatural rain. The sound of rainfall thickened into an endless roar. What began as scattered droplets became an uninterrupted deluge. The liquid no longer seemed to fall from the sky—it descended like flowing curtains, vast invisible veils isolating the world beyond and trapping me within a place that was steadily losing all logic. “Damn it, if we’re leaving then leave now! Why are you just standing there? Weren’t you the one who wanted to go so badly?” She suddenly spoke, startling me. And secondly—wasn’t she the one sitting there resting this whole time? Now she was blaming me? Maybe there had been something I hadn’t heard. Should I just swallow my anger? The water around our feet continued to rise. First it swallowed the soles of our shoes, then our ankles. The freezing sting made it difficult to keep balance, and I suspected walking would soon become impossible altogether. Everything around us warped beneath the rising liquid. “Fine, fine! Let’s go!” I looked upward again. The cloud layer had completely lost its original form. It no longer resembled clouds at all, but rather an enormous mass slowly turning over itself, dark veins writhing within it. And at last, I realized— This was not rain. Then, in the distance, the flood began. “Th-This is...” “Forget walking—run, no matter what!” I could barely move, but I still had to come up with some kind of plan. Then, without hesitation, I wrapped myself tightly around the trunk of a tree. Watching me, she copied the idea and clung to another nearby. Moments later, the flood of dihydrogen monoxide descended upon us. The force of the current was even more violent than I had imagined, like countless freezing wet hands trying to tear me away from the trunk. My fingertips scraped painfully against the rough bark, and I could feel liquid seeping into the wounds. I saw her struggling against another tree not far away, her movements clumsy and panicked. Her coat whipped wildly in the torrent like a dead leaf on the verge of being carried off. The terror in her eyes formed a cruel contrast against the arrogance she had shown before. I shouted at her to hold on tighter, but my voice was instantly devoured by the roaring chaos around us. I couldn’t even tell whether she heard my warning or whether she was simply drowning alone inside her own fear. The water kept rising. Those murky currents were now filled with shattered branches and unidentifiable debris rushing past my sides. Every time something heavy slammed into the tree I clung to, the entire trunk shuddered violently. I could already feel my legs going numb. I tried to see whether the water level was dropping at all, but there was nothing beyond it—only endless water. Its sheer volume rivaled a tsunami. The disaster seemed endless. Time stretched unbearably beneath such relentless tension, and every second spent enduring it felt like burning away the remaining years of my life. From the corner of my eye, I saw her silhouette sway violently. Instinctively, I wanted to reach out toward her, only to realize I could barely save myself. Amid the chaos, I felt the ground beneath the roots collapse with terrifying force—the unmistakable sign of the foundation being hollowed out beneath us. I realized I couldn’t continue clinging to this tree forever. The moment it toppled, I’d be dragged under and crushed beneath the flood with no chance of resurfacing. I had to move. I had to find higher ground before everything vanished beneath the water, even if it meant letting go of the only thing keeping me alive. I released my aching hands. Instantly, the current hurled my body forward. Rainwater and sweat blurred my vision until survival instinct was all I had left, thrashing desperately through that thick liquid. The force of the flood was beyond anything I’d expected. I felt like a marble being violently ricocheted from one impact to another. Murky water poured constantly into my nose and mouth, starving my brain of oxygen. I blinked hard. Just as I tried to avoid a massive floating log, the current twisted suddenly into a grotesque whirlpool that flung my body sideways, stripping away all sense of direction. The collision came without warning. I slammed into a massive tree trunk suspended across the floodwaters. In that instant, agony exploded through my skull alongside a burst of blinding white light. My thoughts dissolved into fragmented flashes like scenes from a dying dream. Just before losing consciousness, I managed to grab onto a piece of drifting wood. And in the edge of my fading vision, I saw myself being swept farther, farther, farther away.





































































[[File:fllppr.png|590px|link=]]





{{User:A4f7/8new|If by life you were deceived, Don't be dismal, don't be wild! In the day of grief, be mild Merry days will come, believe. Heart is living in tomorrow; Present is dejected here; In a moment, passes sorrow; That which passes will be dear.|Alexander Pushkin, If Life Deceived You}} My hands were drenched in blood, and my body ached unbearably. Slowly, I forced myself awake from sleep. Faced with confusion, I could only feel confused, because I had never expected that I hadn’t drowned beneath the collapsed earth. Instead, my wrist remained tightly fixed in place. Survival, then—I supposed my luck had not completely abandoned me. Yet the water still surrounded me. I no longer needed to lift my feet with effort; they could no longer touch the ground. I let my body drift along the dark surface of the water. All I could do was repeatedly confirm that I could still breathe, still open my eyes. The water level no longer seemed to rise, but neither did it recede. I rested my forehead against the muddy stone wall, letting my soaked hair cling to my cheeks. Suddenly, I began imagining an ending where I would remain trapped here forever, soaking endlessly in this water. The thought startled me. I don’t know how much more time passed before I finally noticed the current slowing. The water that had once submerged most of everything gradually retreated bit by bit, revealing more muddy ground and fractured stones. I still didn’t dare stand immediately. I simply stared quietly at the slowly exposed earth, as though witnessing some long-delayed proof. Only when my feet could finally touch the bottom again did I truly believe that I had survived long enough for the floodwaters to withdraw. I walked and walked, following the place I had guided myself toward. The unfinished checklist seemed to urge other creatures onward now; no blade was pointed at me anymore. And then I continued. I saw a convenience store. It was blue, and green. Beside it stretched vibrant green grass, and the sight displeased me. How long had it been since anyone came to this place? For a moment, it seemed to flicker with familiarity. I thought I had seen it before. I still wasn’t sure—had this place changed? Perhaps my eyes were simply exhausted. The automatic door chimed open, its melody sounding like several songs overlapping at once. As I counted the shelves, the fluorescent lights buzzed rhythmically overhead, gradually syncing with that frequency. As if being controlled, I drifted toward the aisles. “You’re still alive?” I heard her again. And I saw her too. Yet in her heart, she didn’t seem particularly happy about it. Just as I had expected. We walked together, soon stopping before the refrigerated section. Our eyes wandered over rows of brightly packaged liquids saturated with artificial coloring, and from the very bottom shelf we hooked out two of the cheapest carbonated drinks. Even though there was no clerk to be found anywhere, we were probably just used to doing this. The bottle caps twisted open, and the long-trapped gas burst out eagerly. That hollow exhaustion from surviving disaster finally peeled itself away from my soul. Did it feel wonderful? Not exactly. “We survived.” She spoke to me again, slowly and deliberately, the corners of her lips twisting upward. “See? The filthy water, all of that, that nightmare... it’s over now. As long as we never speak of it, as long as we pretend nothing ever happened, nobody will know. It’ll rot forever at the bottom of our hearts.” When she finished speaking, she slowly—hesitantly—extended the same hand that had once been stained with wood splinters toward me. It was a gesture filled with reconciliation, as though if I simply took her hand, we could smooth over every argument, betrayal, and breakdown from the past few hours. A nearly pathetic hope flickered in her eyes, trying to use the last remnants of warmth between us to reconnect the two accomplices abandoned by fate—to make peace again, as if this entire tragedy had never happened. This had to be regret. “We’re best friends again, right?” “Yeah. The best,” I answered with a smile. Outside the store, the rain had quietly stopped at some point. What an enormous silence remained behind it. All I could hear was my heartbeat. Slowly, I pushed my weary body upright. I rose gracefully, turned toward the exit, and left the store, my footsteps probably very light. “Now that everything’s over... what are you planning to do next? Or rather, do you still have any goals left?” Oh. She followed after me. Should I answer? “Someone’s gotta clean up this world eventually. I guess my future goal is to become a janitor. I can’t be a doctor anyway.” “Then what exactly are you going to clean?” “Hmm... human trash, maybe?” She burst out laughing. Then I did too, as though someone had struck a hidden nerve in me. The two of us doubled over laughing. “That’s a great goal.” “It really is.” “You’ll probably achieve it soon enough.” “So, since the goal’s already clear, shouldn’t we start practicing first?” “What?” So I drew the gun.
